title: Apply: Why have a civil dialogue?
description: Apply your understanding of why it is necessary to have a civil dialogue.
topic_path: Civil Dialogue, The Constitution, and Constitutional Interpretation
Apply: Why have a civil dialogue?

assessment_items.question_content:
In their civics class, Ha-yoon and her classmate, Luciana, debate whether the government should tax soft drinks and candy to promote more nutritious habits. Ha-yoon claims that the government has a responsibility to protect its citizens and should tax unhealthy foods and drinks to improve overall health. Luciana notes that Ha-yoon made some good points, but argues that taxes on foods and drinks give the government too much power and discourage individual choice. At the end of their debate, Ha-yoon and Luciana shake hands and agree to disagree. 

**This scenario is an example of which of the following practices?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: political interview
data collection
civil dialogue
literature review

assessment_items.question_content:
Before having a constitutional discussion in his civics class, Mr. Thomas asks his students to come up with a set of rules to generate civil dialogue. 

**Which rule would accomplish the goal of having a productive constitutional conversation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Participants must use a civil and respectful tone.
Do not use current examples.
Participants may speak only once.
Mr. Thomas will assign viewpoints to students.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Zuniga, a civics teacher, asks students to prepare materials and arguments for a debate about whether the death penalty is consistent with the Eighth Amendment. 

**What is the most important action a student could take to help them craft an effective constitutional argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Practice what they are going to say to make sure their argument is heard
Read arguments from constitutional scholars about the issue
Focus only on scholarly materials that support the side of the debate that you prefer
Watch crime shows on television to learn how to speak like a lawyer

assessment_items.question_content:
Gregory and Esther are having a discussion about whether voter ID laws are constitutional. Gregory believes that these laws are consistent with the Constitution. He argues, "Within our constitutional system, states have the power to determine the manner of elections. Therefore, these laws are constitutional." Esther disagrees.

**Which statement is the best example of an effective and civil response to Gregory’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: "You don't care about people who don't have a form of identification!"
"It’s wrong to require identification to vote as voting is a right of citizenship that should not be infringed upon."
"I disagree, and I don't want to talk to you anymore."
"The federal government has placed restrictions on deciding how states conduct elections in the past."

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement is an example of cynicism in a civil dialogue?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: “None of this matters anyways.”
“This is a complex issue that we need more information about before we can address it effectively.”
“Let’s agree to disagree.”
“This act is an example of an abuse of power.”

assessment_items.question_content:
In a classroom discussion about policy, Rina says, “The government needs to spend more money to help people who did not make a living wage.” Paul disagrees with Rina. 

**Which statement could Paul make to challenge Rina’s argument without being cynical?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: “People who need assistance from the government are just lazy and are taking advantage of hard-working Americans.”
“The policy that you’re proposing isn’t a long-term solution, and there are several reports that show that this approach can do more harm than good.”
“The government does not care about people and wouldn’t know how best to spend that money anyway.”
“The likelihood of a new policy passing both houses of Congress is relatively low as both parties have rejected similar policies in the past.”

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Federalist No. 1 by Alexander Hamilton.**

This doctrine will, in all probability, be gradually [[☃ definition 1]], till it has [[☃ definition 2]] enough to countenance an open avowal of it. For nothing can be more evident, to those who are able to take an enlarged view of the subject, than the alternative of an adoption of the new Constitution or a dismemberment of the Union. It will therefore be of use to begin by examining the advantages of that Union, the certain evils, and the probable dangers, to which every State will be exposed from its dissolution. This shall accordingly constitute the subject of my next address.

**According to Hamilton, what is the purpose of the Federalist Papers?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To explain why the American colonies should rebel against Great Britain
To convince those in power that there needs to be a constitutional convention
To question political leaders and their beliefs in government
To argue for the ratification of the Constitution and respond to critics

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Federalist No. 1 by Alexander Hamilton.**

This doctrine will, in all probability, be gradually [[☃ definition 1]], till it has [[☃ definition 2]] enough to countenance an open avowal of it. For nothing can be more evident, to those who are able to take an enlarged view of the subject, than the alternative of an adoption of the new Constitution or a dismemberment of the Union. It will therefore be of use to begin by examining the advantages of that Union, the certain evils, and the probable dangers, to which every State will be exposed from its dissolution. This shall accordingly constitute the subject of my next address.

**Based on the excerpt, the Federalist Papers are an example of which of the following?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A strategy to gain political support for a campaign for public office by those in Hamilton’s political party
A series of essays making recommendations for improving the Constitution
An approach to discussing the new constitution to educate people on its features
A presentation of ideas about the legitimacy of a monarchy and hereditary privilege

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Federalist No. 1 by Alexander Hamilton.**

Among the most [[☃ definition 1]] of the obstacles which the new Constitution will have to encounter may readily be distinguished the obvious interest of a certain class of men in every State to resist all changes which may [[☃ definition 5]] a [[☃ definition 2]] of the power, [[☃ definition 3]], and consequence of the offices they hold under the State establishments; and the perverted ambition of another class of men, who will either hope to [[☃ definition 4]] themselves by the confusions of their country, or will flatter themselves with fairer prospects of elevation from the subdivision of the empire into several partial confederacies than from its union under one government.

**According to the excerpt, what is one fear that Hamilton has?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: advantages, such as money or perks, that one gains by taking on an official job
increase the power or reputation of

choices.content: People involved in discussions about the Constitution will act in self-interest.
No one cares about the ratification of the Constitution.
People are too uneducated to understand the proposals in the Constitution.
There are too many conflicting opinions in debates about the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Noah Webster’s “A Citizen of America: An Examination into the Leading Principles of America.” (1787)**

But the origin of the AMERICAN REPUBLIC is distinguished by peculiar circumstances. Other nations have been driven together by fear and necessity—the governments have generally been the result of a single man’s observations; or the offspring of particular interests. In the formation of our constitution, the wisdom of all ages is collected—the legislators of [[☃ definition 1]] are consulted—as well as the opinions and interests of the millions who are concerned. In short, in it *an empire of reason.*

In the formation of such a government, it is not only the *right,* but the [[☃ definition 2]] *duty* of every citizen to examine the principles of it, to compare them with the principles of other governments, with a constant eye to our particular situation and circumstances, and thus [[☃ definition 3]] to foresee the future operations of our own system, and its effects upon human happiness.

**Which statement best summarizes Webster’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: necessary; not able to be ignored

choices.content: Every citizen must agree to implement the new Constitution, as it is based on the philosophies of the most intelligent people from ancient and modern times.
Every citizen must use rational and unemotional arguments in their discussions of what an ideal government looks like.
Every citizen must understand the principles underlying America’s government, which was founded on the collective knowledge of wise people.
Every citizen must study the philosophies of the great thinkers of history to determine what sort of government the United States should have.

assessment_items.question_content:
There is a town hall meeting where citizens debate a proposed new zoning law. The law would allow for the construction of a large apartment complex in an area that is currently zoned only for single-family homes. Tempers are rising. People in favor of the laws argue the complex will address the town's affordable housing shortage. Opponents worry about increased traffic and decreased property values. One resident shouts, "You only care about lining the pockets of real estate developers!" Another resident replies, "You just want to keep out anyone who isn't rich!"

**Which of these approaches would BEST promote more civil and productive dialogue in this situation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The moderator should immediately silence the disruptive residents and insist on strict time limits for speakers to maintain order.
The moderator should remind everyone that the zoning decision has already been made and that the meeting’s purpose is simply to inform residents of the details.
The moderator should encourage residents to express their anger and frustration freely, as this will help them feel heard and validated.
The moderator should acknowledge the residents' concerns but redirect the conversation toward areas of agreement and potential compromises.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In this essay, titled “Cato II” and published in 1787, the author discusses the proposed U.S. Constitution. The author was an Antifederalist and opposed the United States adopting the new U.S. Constitution. In this essay, he responds to a Federalist essay written by a person using the pseudonym Caesar. In Caesar’s essay, the author calls for the automatic approval of the Constitution.**

It is not my view to rouse your passions. I only wish to excite you to, and assist you in, a cool and [[☃ definition 1]] discussion of the subject, to urge you to behave like sensible freemen. Think, speak, act, and assert your opinions and rights—let the same good sense govern you with respect to the adoption of a future system for the administration of your public affairs that influenced you in the formation of the present. . . . My [[☃ definition 2]] is to take up this new form of national government—compare it with the experience and the opinions of the most sensible and approved political authors. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes Cato’s request for the American people in the essay?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To approve the Constitution and implement it quickly to get the new government running as soon as possible
To analyze the Constitution and use their judgment to decide whether to adopt it
To influence the American people to reduce the powers of the federal government
To coax members of militias to arrest the people who wrote the new constitution for betraying the government
